title: ⚡ Electric Vehicle Training
description: Master electric vehicle technology, charging infrastructure, battery management, and EV-specific safety protocols for modern sustainable transport.

units.title: EV Technology Overview
units.content:
# EV Technology Overview

## Types of Electric Vehicles

### Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)
- Fully electric, no internal combustion engine
- Powered solely by battery pack
- Must be plugged in to charge
- Zero tailpipe emissions
- Examples: Tesla Model 3, Nissan Leaf

### Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)
- Combines electric motor with petrol engine
- Can plug in to charge battery
- Electric range typically 30-50 km
- Switches to petrol for longer trips

### Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)
- Cannot be plugged in
- Battery charges through regenerative braking
- Electric motor assists petrol engine
- No external charging needed

## Key Components

### Electric Motor
- Converts electrical energy to mechanical energy
- Provides instant torque
- Fewer moving parts than combustion engine
- Requires no oil changes

### Battery Pack
- Stores electrical energy
- Lithium-ion technology
- Largest and heaviest component
- Located under vehicle floor

### Power Electronics
- Converts DC battery power to AC for motor
- Manages energy flow
- Controls charging
- Includes inverter and converter

## Benefits of EVs
- Lower operating costs
- Reduced maintenance
- Instant torque and smooth acceleration
- Quiet operation
- Zero tailpipe emissions

## Common Myths
- EVs run out of charge quickly (Range 300-500 km typical)
- EV batteries need frequent replacement (8-10 year warranty)
- EVs are slow (Many accelerate faster than petrol cars)
- Charging takes too long (Fast charge 30-45 minutes)
key_points: BEVs are fully electric with zero emissions
EVs have instant torque and fewer moving parts
Battery packs have 8-10 year warranties
Typical EV range is 300-500 km per charge
EVs have lower operating and maintenance costs

units.title: EV Controls & Dashboard
units.content:
# EV Controls & Dashboard

## Key Dashboard Displays

### Battery Charge Indicator
- Shows current battery level as percentage
- Visual bar graph representation
- Color coding (green=good, yellow=low, red=critical)
- Located prominently on display

### Range Estimate
- Shows estimated remaining distance
- Calculated based on recent driving
- Affected by driving style and conditions
- May show both city and highway range

### Energy Consumption
- Real-time consumption display (kWh/100km)
- Average consumption over trip
- Instantaneous usage indicator
- Helps optimize driving efficiency

### Regenerative Braking Indicator
- Shows when regen is active
- Indicates energy being recovered
- Bar graph showing regen strength
- Helps understand efficiency

## EV-Specific Controls

### Regenerative Braking Settings
- Adjustable regen levels (Low/Medium/High)
- One-pedal driving mode
- Coasting mode selection
- Accessed through drive mode selector

### Charging Port Release
- Button to open charge port door
- Located on dashboard or key fob
- May have manual release inside
- Pre-heat/cool battery option

### Pre-conditioning
- Climate control while charging
- Set departure time for optimal battery
- Remote activation via app
- Uses grid power, not battery

## Warning Lights
- Battery temperature warning (red = overheating)
- Charging system fault
- Electric drive system warning
- High-voltage system warning
- Reduced power mode indicator
key_points: Monitor battery percentage and range estimate
Use energy consumption display to optimize driving
Adjust regenerative braking to preference
Pre-condition while plugged in to preserve range
Understand EV-specific warning lights

units.title: Battery Management & Range Optimization
units.content:
# Battery Management & Range Optimization

## Optimal Charging Practices

### Daily Charging
- Keep battery between 20% and 80% for daily driving
- Avoid frequent 100% charges
- Set charge limit in vehicle settings
- Plug in when parked if possible
- Battery management system prevents overcharging

### Long Trip Charging
- Charge to 100% before long trips
- Time completion with departure
- Use fast chargers for en-route charging
- Plan charging stops every 2-3 hours
- Target 10-80% for fast charging (optimal speed)

### Extreme Temperature Considerations
- Battery performance reduced in cold
- Pre-condition while plugged in
- Park in garage or shade when hot
- Avoid charging when battery extremely hot or cold
- Battery cooling system activates automatically

## Factors Affecting Range

### Driving Style
- Aggressive acceleration reduces range by 30%+
- Smooth driving maximizes efficiency
- High speeds (>100 km/h) reduce range
- Use cruise control on highways
- Anticipate stops to coast

### Environmental Conditions
- Cold weather reduces range 20-40%
- Hot weather reduces range 10-20%
- Headwinds reduce range
- Rain and wet roads increase resistance
- Mountainous terrain reduces range

### Vehicle Load
- Every 100 kg reduces range ~5%
- Roof racks reduce range significantly
- Ensure proper tyre pressure
- Remove unnecessary cargo
- Closed windows reduce drag

## Range Anxiety Management
- Plan trips using charging apps
- Know locations of fast chargers
- Keep buffer of 50 km minimum
- Slow down if range is tight
- Reduce climate control usage
- Use eco driving mode

## Battery Health
- Avoid frequent fast charging
- Don't leave at 100% for extended periods
- Don't let battery sit at 0%
- Use scheduled charging
- Battery warranty typically 8 years/160,000 km
key_points: Keep battery between 20-80% for daily driving
Aggressive driving reduces range by 30%+
Cold weather reduces range 20-40%
Plan trips with charging stops every 2-3 hours
Maintain 50 km range buffer

units.title: Charging Infrastructure
units.content:
# Charging Infrastructure

## Charging Levels

### Level 1 (Standard Outlet)
- 120V household outlet
- 8-16 km of range per hour
- Full charge: 40-60 hours
- Suitable for overnight charging
- No installation required

### Level 2 (Home/Public)
- 240V (similar to dryer outlet)
- 30-50 km of range per hour
- Full charge: 6-10 hours
- Most common home charging solution
- Professional installation recommended

### DC Fast Charging (Level 3)
- 400V+ commercial chargers
- 150-500+ km of range per hour
- 10-80% charge in 30-45 minutes
- Only for long trips
- More expensive than Level 2

## Charging Networks in Kenya

### Public Charging Locations
- Shopping malls (Two Rivers, Garden City, Village Market)
- Hotels (Sankara, Radisson Blu)
- Office buildings
- Petrol stations
- Airport parking

### Charging Apps
- PlugShare (find stations worldwide)
- Kenya Power EV charging map
- ChargeNet Kenya
- Company-specific apps (EVChaja)

## Home Charging Installation
- Assess electrical panel capacity
- Hire certified electrician
- Install dedicated circuit
- Consider time-of-use rates
- Apply for any permits

## Charging Etiquette
- Don't leave car plugged in when full
- Move car promptly after charging
- Don't block chargers if not charging
- Be courteous to other EV drivers
- Report broken chargers

## Costs
- Level 2 home: KES 300-500 per full charge
- DC fast charging: KES 1,000-2,000 per full charge
- Time-of-use rates may offer cheaper overnight charging
- Some public chargers free with parking
key_points: Level 1: 8-16 km/hour (40-60 hours full charge)
Level 2: 30-50 km/hour (6-10 hours full charge)
DC Fast: 150-500+ km/hour (30-45 minutes to 80%)
Use apps to locate charging stations
Don't occupy chargers when not charging

units.title: Regenerative Braking
units.content:
# Regenerative Braking

## How Regenerative Braking Works
- Electric motor acts as generator during deceleration
- Kinetic energy converted to electrical energy
- Energy stored back in battery
- Reduces wear on friction brakes
- Extends driving range

## Regenerative Braking Modes

### Low Regen
- Mild deceleration when lifting throttle
- Feels similar to coasting in petrol car
- More friction braking needed
- Better for highway driving

### Medium Regen
- Moderate deceleration
- Good balance for mixed driving
- Recovers moderate energy
- Default setting for many EVs

### High Regen
- Strong deceleration when lifting throttle
- Enables one-pedal driving
- Maximum energy recovery
- Best for city driving

## One-Pedal Driving
- Car slows significantly when lifting throttle
- Can come to complete stop without brake pedal
- Requires practice to master
- Maximizes energy recovery
- Reduces brake pad wear

## Maximizing Energy Recovery
- Anticipate stops to coast
- Lift throttle early for traffic lights
- Use regen instead of friction brakes
- Descend hills in high regen mode
- Smooth, gradual deceleration is best

## Limitations
- Regen less effective at low speeds
- May be reduced when battery is full
- Less effective in slippery conditions
- Friction brakes still needed for emergency stops
- Regen doesn't work if battery is too cold

## Safety Considerations
- Regen may feel unfamiliar initially
- Practice in safe area
- Be aware regen may disengage with ABS
- Use friction brakes for emergency stops
- Explain regen to others driving your EV
key_points: Regenerative braking recovers energy and extends range
High regen mode enables one-pedal driving
Lift throttle early to maximize energy recovery
Regen less effective at low speeds or full battery
Practice one-pedal driving in safe area first

units.title: EV Maintenance & Safety
units.content:
# EV Maintenance & Safety

## No Oil Changes
- Electric motors don't use engine oil
- No oil changes needed
- No oil filters to replace
- Significant maintenance savings
- Check owner's manual for specific requirements

## Regular Maintenance Items

### Tyre Rotation and Pressure
- Same as conventional vehicles
- Check monthly
- Rotate every 10,000 km
- Proper inflation crucial for range
- EVs are heavier, tyres wear faster

### Brake System
- Less wear due to regenerative braking
- Brake fluid changes every 2-3 years
- Brake pad inspection annually
- Caliper maintenance for rust prevention
- Friction brakes still needed

### Cabin Air Filter
- Replace every 15,000-30,000 km
- Same as conventional vehicles
- Affects HVAC performance
- Easy DIY replacement

### Coolant System
- Battery and motor cooling
- Check levels regularly
- Replace per manufacturer schedule
- Use correct coolant type

### Windshield Washer Fluid
- Same as conventional vehicles
- Check monthly
- Refill as needed

## High-Voltage Safety

### Orange Cables
- Identify high-voltage cables (bright orange)
- Never touch damaged orange cables
- Stay clear during accidents
- Only qualified technicians should handle

### Emergency Response
- EV may be silent when on
- Cut 12V battery first (usually under hood)
- Avoid cutting orange cables
- Don't touch damaged high-voltage components
- Fire requires special extinguisher

## What to Do in an Accident
- Ensure vehicle is in PARK
- Exit vehicle if safe
- Move away from vehicle
- Warn emergency responders it's an EV
- Don't approach if you see orange cables exposed

## Maintenance Schedule
- Annual safety inspection
- Battery health check (yearly)
- Coolant flush (every 5 years/100,000 km)
- Brake fluid change (every 3 years)
- Gearbox oil (every 100,000 km)
key_points: EVs require no oil changes - significant savings
Tyre rotation every 10,000 km (EVs are heavier)
Never touch damaged orange high-voltage cables
Warn emergency responders vehicle is EV after accident
Brake fluid changes still needed every 2-3 years

units.title: Eco-Driving Techniques
units.content:
# Eco-Driving Techniques

## Smooth Acceleration
- Avoid rapid acceleration
- Use gentle throttle inputs
- Smooth acceleration uses less energy
- Plan moves to avoid unnecessary speed changes
- Use eco mode when available

## Anticipatory Driving
- Look ahead to anticipate stops
- Coast to traffic lights
- Avoid last-minute braking
- Maintain steady speed on highways
- Use regenerative braking

## Speed Management

### Optimal Speed
- Most efficient speed: 50-80 km/h
- Efficiency drops significantly above 100 km/h
- Every 8 km/h above 100 reduces range ~10%
- Slower in city, moderate on highway

### Highway Driving
- Use cruise control for steady speed
- Reduce speed to extend range
- Drafting behind trucks (safe distance only)
- Close windows to reduce drag

## Climate Control Usage

### Heating
- Uses significant energy
- Pre-heat while plugged in
- Use seat heaters instead of cabin heat
- Lower temperature setting
- Consider heated steering wheel

### Air Conditioning
- Uses moderate energy
- Use recirculation mode
- Pre-cool while plugged in
- Park in shade when possible
- Lower fan speed

## Vehicle Preparation
- Remove roof racks when not in use
- Remove unnecessary weight
- Check tyre pressure monthly
- Use recommended tyre type
- Keep vehicle clean (less drag)

## Trip Planning
- Combine short trips
- Avoid peak traffic
- Use navigation to find efficient routes
- Pre-heat/cool while plugged in
- Charge to 80% for daily driving

## Eco Driving Score
- Many EVs provide driving score
- Monitor efficiency feedback
- Set efficiency goals
- Compare with other drivers
- Improve over time
key_points: Smooth acceleration and anticipation maximize range
Most efficient speed is 50-80 km/h
Pre-heat/cool cabin while plugged in
Remove roof racks and unnecessary weight
Monitor eco driving score to improve efficiency
